sentences.text: We are coming now to the year 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were banished from Krakow, myself, my mother, and my sister, because the German did not want to tolerate any Jews in Krakow itself.
So we had to move to the suburbs of Krakow.
At that time, it was about five miles, six miles from Krakow itself.
It was a little village called Borek [?
Wielkopolski, ?] Borek, B-O-R-E-K.
When the Germans invaded Russia June 22, 1941, right away, they herded us back to Krakow, but not to our apartments, but to a ghetto.
They established a ghetto.
They walled off a portion [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of a Krakow borough, which was called Podgolrze, P-O-D-G-O-- with a [INAUDIBLE]-- R-Z-E, Podgolrze.
You mean in the village?
Yes.
There were Jews who-- who took residence up-- who had to take up residence there, rent-- renting rooms from Poles who had spare rooms.
No, no, no, no.
Many of our possessions just remained because we could-- what-- what could you put in one little room?
We had one little room, myself, my sister, my-- and my-- my mother, one little room in-- in that village.
Of course, of course.
They were watching.
Everybody was watching, especially the Poles.

They were trying to take advantage by selling us double the price produce and taking advantage in such a way that they should-- they should get as much profit as possible from the-- from the misfortune of the Jews.
Yes, there were my cousins were in-- in Borek, some of my friends, and-- and a rabbi also was there, whom I adored, whom I studied with.
His name was Levertov, Levertov, L-E-V-E-R-T-O-V, Levertov.
And he and his family was there, also living in a-- in a room.
I just helped out my uncle in some of his dealings with the Poles.
He sold them some yarns, you know.
There was still some possibility of-- of little-- little peddling, I would say, so he was a peddler-- became a peddler.
Because--
His name was Ignac Silbiger.
Ignac Silbiger, you know, the-- I-- I spelled the name before.
I worked with him, helping him out carry here, carry there, little chores.
But this was a transitionally-- transition period for us because then, our main residence was now in the ghetto in June.
You mean in-- in the village?
Yes, to-- to some extent.
Not too much because it was also-- the services were held in-- in little rooms of the rabbis, was it.
And not too much, no.
No.
Under the watchful eyes of the Poles, how can you-- how can you feel?
How can you say your devotions and-- and have-- have [INAUDIBLE] and your prayers when you are being watched by-- by hate-- hateful eyes?
Yes, I remember Pesach at that time.
All we had to eat was potatoes, mostly potatoes.
Very little matzo.
There was some matzo, but it was-- it was expensive because there-- there-- there were not any bakeries, and when it was baked, it was baked illegally.
You see?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Pesach was very uneventful.
Nobody looked forward to-- to-- to good food or to-- to-- to wine or something.
Wine was made from raisins, you know, some fermentation with raisins.
Homemade wine, which tasted OK, but was not really wine, was-- it was wine for-- for the purpose of making a benediction over it, but it really didn't taste like wine.
I remember.
Very much so, more-- more than in any other time, especially comes to mind when I walked with my mother in a street in Borek.
A pack of the [INAUDIBLE], a pack of-- of Polish teenagers came over and demanded money from my mother, but she said, I didn't take any money.
So they lit a cigarette, and-- and they burned my mother with a cigarette, with a-- with a lighted cigarette.
And she was screaming from-- from pain, and I-- and I tried to-- to-- I don't know what I tried.
I tried to fend-- to fend them off, to protect her, and they beat me up.
It was a terrible, terrible experience that's stuck in my mind.
Did they say anything to you during--?
No.
They called me dirty Jew.
That's all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so-- this was routine, a routine call, a dirty Jew.
Well, they left after they-- they saw that my mother didn't have any money, didn't take any money, didn't have any money.
So they left, but not before subjecting her to such terrible pain and indignity, and me too, of course.
But--
Happy, laughing, laughter.

[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] The former prime minister of Israel-- his name was Shamir, Yitzhak Shamir-- who was-- who was-- who is-- who is of Polish ancestry said that the Poles drank the hatred of the Jew together with their mothers' milk.
This is what he pronounced publicly, really.
And of course, immediately, there were protests from Poland, so he somehow wiggled out of it.
But he knew what he was talking about.
This is what stuck-- quite possibly, but what stuck in my mind was this incident-- particular incident.
Oh, yes, especially by the Germans.
You see, the Germans did dirty work for the Poles.
They cut half of the beard of the Jews, or they cut the entire beard entirely, with-- with-- not even with scissors.
They had some kind of-- some-- some blades.
Like-- it was very painful.
In Krakow.
In Krakow.
Nothing.
They just were hiding.
They saw the Germans having fun, so they were hiding.
People were hiding, running away because there was no possibility to-- to fight back.
I mean, it was an invitation for suicide.
Yes.
Trembling and begging for-- for-- you know, but it was such fright, such fear, that it numbed this person.
It was a numbing fear.
The German commandant-- a numbing fear till the very end.
Because of their tremendous personal impact of their deportment, they had an aura of terror around them.
And people who did not have any weapons, who did not have anything to fight back with, just were afraid, frightened, terrified.
Not very much, because it was dangerous.
If not the Germans, it was the Poles.
It was dangerous.
People were being taken, especially intelligentsia people, you see, people of higher standing, like doctors, or engineers, or people of-- of prominent-- of prominence.
The Germans knew about them, and they had provided-- they were provided with lists of such people.
Many of such people were taken away to-- to-- to Auschwitz right away, right at that time.
I heard about people taken away.
I heard of them because it was done during the night.
Yes.
We-- we hired a horse and buggy, a Polish horse and buggy.
And whatever possessions we had, we loaded the-- loaded on the horse and buggy, and we went into the ghetto.
It was in July 1941.
July '41.
My father was still in Lemberg.
But when the Germans conquered Lemberg, or they went into Lemberg, he knew that he has to be under-- under German occupation now.
He did not run away with Russians.
He didn't-- he did not move to the east with the Russians, but he remained in Lemberg.
So he joined us somehow by-- by bribing some-- some Germans.
They took him on, and they deposited him in-- in the ghetto of Krakow, and he joined us in the ghetto of Krakow.
We had a room which was about, I would say, 20 feet by 20 feet for my father, my mother, my sister, and myself, four people.
We didn't-- There was nowhere to move, even, let alone to-- to-- to sit at a table.
This was ghetto.
In one apartment-- there was one apartment-- there were four families living in one apartment of three rooms and the kitchen, yes.
It was-- I know the name or the-- the name of the street.
Jozefinska, Jozefinska Street.
Jozefinska, J-O-Z-E-F-I-N-S-K-Y-- S-K-A-- A-- S-K-A, Jozefinska, [?
J ?] over the-- over the-- apostrophe.
This was the main street of the ghetto at that time.
And as I said before, we-- we were three families in an apartment.
Did I say four families?
I think four families in the apartment, yes.
There were four families in the apartment, with children also.
We had one bathroom.
One-- It was not a bathroom.
It was a-- a-- it wasn't really a bathroom.
It was just a commode.
There were no bathrooms, just a commode for about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nine families on one floor, and everybody was standing in line to get-- to-- to be able to be in the commode.
No showers, of course.
At that time-- in the ghetto, there were no showers, really.
Whenever we wanted to take a bath, we had to find a pail.
We sat down, and-- and somebody poured water over us.
This was the-- this was the bath taking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The conditions in the ghetto became worse and worse progressively.
There was enough bread, I would say.
Bread, some vegetables, limited quantities, very limited.
I know that I was many times hungry.
I could not-- I didn't have enough food.
I was many times hungry in the ghetto.
But it was not the worst yet.
The worst was yet to come.
But the conditions became worse and worse.
Different from before that I had to work for the Germans four times a week-- of course, without pay-- four times a week.
Different-- a different work commandos, different workplaces, where they sent us out.
You never knew where-- where-- where-- we had to report to a work-- work detachment, and the Germans took us on a truck, and we didn't know where we were going.
They were deciding where we are going to go to-- to work for about 12 hours a day.
And the work was terrible.
It was-- it was-- They over-- they were overseers.
The Germans were overseers, and they were driving us like-- like slaves.
Like slaves.
The conditions were terrible.
There was no-- there was-- in the 12 hours we had, we had only half an hour for just to rest or eat something, whatever.
There was food available.
I-- I didn't eat because I was trying to observe Kashrut.
So whatever I was able to take from home, a piece of bread or something, I ate at that time during the half-hour break.
Soup.
It's called in German, [GERMAN], meaning the soup had everything-- meat, whatever meat they had-- horse meat.
Whatever was in-- in that bowl of soup that people who did not observe Kashrut ate.
Those who did observe Kashrut had to bring their own food from home.
In the ghetto--
Mostly schlepping things, carrying heavy loads, stones, pails of-- of-- of-- hay, you know, compressed hay, all kinds of-- all kinds of work, and-- and building roads and-- and-- and carrying wood.
You know, menial work, menial work, physical work, all physical work.
Same thing.
He was also taken, mostly with me, see.
So we worked together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The women were-- were taken to shovel snow, see, from the ghetto-- to shovel snow, and to-- they were returned to the ghetto later in the evening, like we, together.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming back home, we were so exhausted that all we can do-- all we could do was just collapse and have some few hours of sleep.
We tried.
I tried to hide on Shabbat in order not to be taken to-- by the Germans to-- to their forced labor outposts.
And the Shabbat-- what can you-- as-- as-- as I said before, we had services at the rabbi's on Shabbat in the ghetto, and most of the time, most of the time, I did not have to work on Shabbat.
I just did all kinds of-- of-- of acrobatics not to-- not to be-- not to have to go out and work on Shabbat.
I know that I-- we bribed somebody.
They should go for health.
We bribed the Germans.
The Germans were very amenable to bribes.
There were some-- you know, some Germans who could be bribed and-- let him go.
Not-- don't-- look away when he's coming on Shabbat.
Don't make any problems.
Very limited amounts, very limited.
It was-- it was really miserable, miserable.
But whatever we had, whatever I had, I was trying to-- to get out, and my father was going-- tried-- tried very hard to get out Shabbat from work.
It was extremely hard.
It was extremely hard.
I was a-- at that time, I was a teenager.
A teenager wants-- wants to have fun, wants to have friends, go out with friends, spend time with-- with equals.
No such thing.
There was no such thing.

Everybody was striving just to have enough food and to-- to save-- to save their lives as-- as much as they could because at that time already, in 1940-- late 1941, people were taken out of ghetto, hundreds of people, to be transported to extermination camps in Belzec, in Treblinka, and in Sobibor.
We-- we knew that the people were taken out of the ghetto and transported somewhere.
We didn't know where yet.
We did not have the information.
We didn't have the terrible news that the people were transported to be killed and-- and-- and burned.
We didn't know it, because the Germans were very adept at hiding, at-- at secrecy.
They were very adept at it.
Because if somebody would get-- would get wind of it, what-- what happened to these people, they would have a rebellion on their hands, like they had in-- in-- in Warsaw.
The Warsaw Rebellion was also a-- a-- in 1943, was a result of people getting wind of what-- what was going on, what-- what-- where-- where-- where the transports were taken.
Yes, we heard because the Poles that had some contact with Jews in the ghetto, they-- they gave us the information in 1943 about the ghetto uprising in-- in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They said, you Jews should be proud that you are fighting back a little bit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, we didn't know what happened.
Just-- we had an inkling, I would say, about an uprising in ghetto Warsaw at that time.
That was '42, '43, I suppose.
Study, pray, wash, do some chores-- I mean, chores for the house, chores for the family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Different-- I mean, there were so many things to do.
After all, I was still a teenager.
Tried to meet some friends, but we-- everybody was preoccupied with himself or herself.
Everybody was preoccupied.
People living together in such a close-- such close quarters-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] remarkably, remarkably, there was friendship.
There was admiration from one-- for one another.
There was the will to survive, the will to-- even the will to observe as many [INAUDIBLE], as many commandments as possible.
There were young men of Hasidic background who were hiding-- who were hiding in the ghetto, somewhere in-- in-- in cellars, and they were just practicing Judaism.
They were studying.
They were praying.
They were occupied only with holiness.
Young-- young men-- Hasidim.

I met occasionally some of these, but they were-- see, they were jeopardizing their lives because if the Germans would find out that they are not-- that they are-- what they are doing, they would immediately shot them or transported them away to camps of extermination.
No, but it was in the neighborhood.
It was, as I said, close quarters.
Everybody knew-- knew about everybody else.
There was like-- like one big family.
Everybody knew what's-- what's-- what's going on.
We had news of the outside world, especially when Polish contacts-- there were some Polish contacts.
They let us know what's going on.
But at that time, the Germans were winning everywhere, so there was a tremendous atmosphere of depression because the Germans were always winning.
They were always conquering.
They were all-- they were close to Moscow.
They're close to already in Caucasus Mountains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It looked like-- and-- and Japan took over south-- Southeast Asia.
Philippines fell.
There was-- this was all shattering news.

Here we are in the ghetto, suffering and-- and-- and vying for some good news, some-- some victories of the Allies, but it was-- was terrible because the-- all the news we heard was that the Germans are here, and they conquered here.
They conquered there.
And they-- they-- they were victorious everywhere in that particular time, 1941, '42.
Well, when we were taken to work, we-- we had to leave the ghetto.
It was outside, and-- and the German posts-- you see, we worked for the Germans.
Wherever they were stationed, they took us to-- to work for them, to build roads, to build houses, to build whatever--
--to schlep.
No, not on our own.
We always left the ghetto in groups, in work groups, labor groups.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were-- there was German police in the ghetto looking out for any trouble, any signs of-- of discontent, or any signs of-- of rebelliousness.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very watchful, extremely watchful.
And if-- if there was any-- a group discussing anything together, they immediately broke up the group, most often with blows over the head.
